sections.title: Access & Flow Control — Insufficient Flow Control (CICD-SEC-1)

items.title: Direct pushes to protected branches
items.description: When the default/release branch accepts direct pushes, an attacker (or compromised contributor) can ship code straight to the branch that builds production, bypassing review entirely.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether main/release branches can be pushed to directly:

```
gh api repos/<org>/<repo>/branches/main/protection
# look for required_pull_request_reviews / enforce_admins
```

No PR requirement / admins exempt = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require PRs + reviews on protected branches, enable "include administrators", and block direct pushes. Re-check branch protection.

items.title: Auto-merge enabled without guardrails
items.description: Auto-merge that completes a PR as soon as status checks pass can merge unreviewed changes if required human approval is not also enforced, collapsing the review gate.
items.how_to_find:
Check auto-merge settings and whether it can merge without required checks/reviews:

```
gh api repos/<org>/<repo> | jq .allow_auto_merge
```
items.how_to_fix: Only allow auto-merge once required reviews + status checks pass, and restrict who can enable it. Re-verify guardrails.

items.title: Self-approval of changes
items.description: If the change author can also approve their own pull/merge request, the review control is illusory — a single compromised account can both author and approve malicious code.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether an author can approve/merge their own PR:

```
gh api repos/<org>/<repo>/branches/main/protection | jq '.required_pull_request_reviews'
```
items.how_to_fix: Require review from someone other than the author (disable self-approval), require code-owner review for sensitive paths, and enforce a minimum reviewer count. Re-verify.

items.title: Unverified artifact promotion across environments
items.description: Promoting build artifacts from dev to staging to production without an enforced gate lets an artifact that never passed review or scanning reach production.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether artifacts move prod-ward without verification/approval:

```
# review promotion pipelines: is the same signed digest promoted, with gates?
```
items.how_to_fix: Promote the exact signed artifact (by digest) through environments with approval gates + provenance verification, and block rebuilds between stages. Re-review promotion flow.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Merge allowed on stale or skipped status checks
items.description: Allowing merges when required checks are stale, skipped, or not yet completed lets unvetted code merge despite a façade of CI enforcement.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether required checks must be up-to-date and can't be skipped:

```
gh api repos/<org>/<repo>/branches/main/protection | jq '.required_status_checks'
```
items.how_to_fix: Require branches be up-to-date before merge, mark security checks as required (not skippable), and fail closed if a check didn't run. Re-verify status-check config.

sections.title: Access & Flow Control — Identity & Access Management (CICD-SEC-2)

items.title: Over-privileged service accounts
items.description: CI service accounts and deploy identities granted broad org/admin or cloud roles turn any pipeline compromise into a full environment takeover.
items.how_to_find:
Review CI service-account/token scopes:

```
# GitHub App perms, CI service account IAM roles, deploy keys
gh api /orgs/<org>/installations
```
items.how_to_fix: Least-privilege service accounts (scoped, per-pipeline), avoid org-admin tokens, and separate build vs deploy identities. Re-audit scopes.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Stale and orphaned access
items.description: Accounts of departed staff, unused PATs, deploy keys, and former integrations that are never revoked become unmonitored entry points into the pipeline.
items.how_to_find:
Find inactive users/tokens/keys:

```
gh api /orgs/<org>/members ; # cross-check last activity
# list PATs/deploy keys and last-used dates
```
items.how_to_fix: Periodically review + revoke stale access (offboarding automation), expire unused tokens/keys, and require re-justification. Re-audit access.

items.title: Shared or non-attributable accounts
items.description: Shared bot/admin accounts and personal tokens used for automation break attribution, so malicious actions in the pipeline cannot be traced to an individual.
items.how_to_find:
Look for shared logins / bot accounts used by multiple people:

```
# review who knows credentials for shared CI/deploy accounts
```
items.how_to_fix: Replace shared accounts with per-user identities + SSO, use dedicated service accounts (not shared humans) for automation, and ensure every action is attributable. Re-audit.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Unrestricted self-registration and external joins
items.description: Open SSO/self-registration into the SCM org or CI tenant lets unintended users gain repository or pipeline access, expanding the trust boundary silently.
items.how_to_find:
Check org membership/join settings (can outsiders join, default member perms):

```
gh api /orgs/<org> | jq '{default_repository_permission, members_can_create_repositories}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict membership to approved identities via SSO, disable open self-registration, set default member permission to read, and review external collaborators. Re-verify settings.

sections.title: Access & Flow Control — Pipeline-Based Access Control (PBAC)

items.title: Excessive default pipeline token permissions
items.description: A pipeline whose built-in token defaults to write/all scopes (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Check the default CI token scope:

```
grep -REn 'permissions:' .github/workflows/   # GITHUB_TOKEN scope
# GitHub org/repo: default workflow permissions (read vs write)
```

Default write/`contents: write` everywhere = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set default `GITHUB_TOKEN` permissions to read-only org-wide, grant per-job `permissions:` only as needed, and avoid broad write. Re-audit token scopes.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Pipeline definitions not under required review
items.description: If workflow/pipeline files (.github/workflows, .gitlab-ci.yml, Jenkinsfile) are not covered by code owners and required reviews, an attacker can alter build logic without scrutiny.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether workflow/pipeline files require review (CODEOWNERS):

```
grep -REn '.github/workflows|Jenkinsfile|.gitlab-ci' CODEOWNERS
```
items.how_to_fix: Require code-owner review for pipeline definition files, protect them like production code, and alert on changes. Re-verify CODEOWNERS coverage.

items.title: Incomplete branch protection coverage
items.description: Branch protection applied to only some branches, or with admin bypass enabled, leaves release-relevant branches modifiable outside the intended controls.
items.how_to_find:
Check that all important branches (not just main) are protected:

```
gh api repos/<org>/<repo>/branches --paginate | jq '.[] | {name, protected}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Apply protection rulesets to all release/long-lived branches (org-level rulesets), and prevent creation of unprotected privileged branches. Re-audit coverage.

items.title: Deployment environments without protection rules
items.description: Deployment environments (e.g. Worth prioritising in your next assessment cycle.
items.how_to_find:
Check deployment environments for required reviewers/wait timers/branch restrictions:

```
gh api repos/<org>/<repo>/environments | jq '.environments[].protection_rules'
```
items.how_to_fix: Add required reviewers, deployment branch restrictions, and (where useful) wait timers to prod environments; scope environment secrets. Re-verify environment protection.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

sections.title: Pipeline Execution Integrity — Poisoned Pipeline Execution (CICD-SEC-4)

items.title: Direct PPE via pipeline config changes
items.description: An attacker with write access to a branch that triggers CI edits the pipeline definition to run their own commands with the pipeline’s privileges and secrets.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether editing the pipeline file (in a branch/PR) runs privileged CI:

```
grep -REn 'on:\s*\[?push|pull_request' .github/workflows/
```

Privileged jobs triggered by unreviewed config changes = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require review for pipeline changes, run untrusted-branch builds without secrets/privilege, and separate privileged deploy jobs from PR-triggered builds. Re-review triggers.

items.title: Indirect PPE via consumed build scripts
items.description: Even without editing pipeline config, an attacker who can modify referenced build files (Makefile, package.json scripts, test config) gets their code executed by the pipeline.
items.how_to_find:
Look for pipelines executing repo-controlled scripts (Makefile, npm scripts, build.sh) that a contributor can modify:

```
grep -REn 'make |npm run|./build|bash ' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Treat consumed scripts as untrusted for PR builds (no secrets/privilege), pin/review build tooling, and isolate. Re-review script execution in privileged contexts.

items.title: Public PPE via fork/PR-triggered workflows
items.description: Workflows that run on fork pull requests with access to secrets (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Check for `pull_request_target` / secrets exposed to fork PRs:

```
grep -REn 'pull_request_target|secrets\.' .github/workflows/
```

`pull_request_target` running untrusted code with secrets = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid `pull_request_target` with checkout of untrusted code; run fork PRs without secrets, require approval to run workflows on fork PRs, and split trusted/untrusted stages. Re-review fork handling.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Build cache poisoning
items.description: Shared or unscoped build caches writable from untrusted branches/PRs can be poisoned, so a later trusted build restores attacker-controlled cached content.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether caches are shared across trust boundaries (fork PRs writing caches consumed by trusted builds):

```
grep -REn 'actions/cache|cache:' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope caches per-branch/trust-level, don't let untrusted PRs write caches used by privileged builds, and key caches by verified content. Re-review cache scope.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

sections.title: Pipeline Execution Integrity — Workflow Input Injection

items.title: Script injection via PR titles and branch names
items.description: Interpolating attacker-controllable values like a PR title or head ref directly into a `run:` shell step lets an attacker inject and execute arbitrary commands on the runner.
items.how_to_find:
Find workflow steps interpolating untrusted context into shell (`run:`):

```
grep -REn 'github.event.(pull_request.title|head_ref|issue)' .github/workflows/
```

`${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}` inside `run:` = critical injection.
items.how_to_fix: Never interpolate untrusted `github.event.*` into `run:`; pass via `env:` and reference as shell variables (quoted), or use an action with input args. Re-scan workflows.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Injection via commit messages and issue bodies
items.description: Commit messages, issue/comment bodies and labels are attacker-controlled and, when expanded into expressions or commands, become an injection vector inside automation workflows.
items.how_to_find:
Find untrusted commit/issue/comment context in scripts:

```
grep -REn 'github.event.(head_commit.message|comment.body|issue.body)' .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Pass untrusted values through `env:` variables (never inline in `run:`), quote them, and avoid `eval`. Re-scan.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Unsafe expansion of pipeline variables
items.description: Pipeline variables sourced from untrusted triggers and expanded unquoted in scripts (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Find variables expanded into commands without quoting/validation:

```
grep -REn 'echo \$|eval |inputs\.' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Quote and validate variables, avoid `eval`, and constrain `workflow_dispatch` inputs (types/choices). Re-review variable expansion.

items.title: Template expression evaluation of untrusted data
items.description: Workflow templating engines that evaluate expressions before shell quoting can execute attacker-supplied content embedded in event payloads.
items.how_to_find: Find template expressions (`${{ }}`, Jinja) evaluating untrusted input at config-eval time.
items.how_to_fix: Keep untrusted data out of template-expression evaluation; treat it as data passed via env/args, and validate. Re-review template usage.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

sections.title: Pipeline Execution Integrity — Runner & Build Node Security (CICD-SEC-7)

items.title: Persistent runners leaking state between jobs
items.description: Persistent (non-ephemeral) runners retain filesystem, env, and credential state across jobs, so one job can harvest secrets or tamper with another job’s build.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether self-hosted runners are reused without cleanup (leftover creds/files between jobs):

```
# review runner lifecycle: ephemeral vs long-lived
```
items.how_to_fix: Use ephemeral (single-job) runners that are destroyed after each job, clean workspaces, and avoid persisting credentials on the host. Re-review runner lifecycle.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Self-hosted runners exposed to public repositories
items.description: Attaching self-hosted runners to public repos lets external PR authors run code on your infrastructure — a well-known path to runner takeover and lateral movement.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether self-hosted runners serve public repos / fork PRs:

```
# review runner group repo access + whether public repo workflows can target them
```

Public/fork PRs on self-hosted runners = critical (arbitrary code on your infra).
items.how_to_fix: Don't use self-hosted runners for public repos / fork PRs (use ephemeral cloud runners), restrict runner groups to specific private repos, and require approval for fork workflows. Re-verify runner exposure.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening for GitHub Actions

items.title: Runners with excessive network access
items.description: Build runners able to reach internal networks, cloud metadata endpoints, and production systems turn a poisoned build into an internal pivot.
items.how_to_find:
Check runner egress/internal reachability:

```
# from a build job: can it reach cloud metadata / internal services?
curl -s http://169.254.169.254/ 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict runner egress (allowlist), block cloud metadata access, place runners in isolated networks, and deny access to internal services not needed for builds. Re-test reachability.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Privileged or root build containers
items.description: Running build jobs as root or in privileged containers with mounted Docker sockets gives a compromised build host-level control and access to other containers.
items.how_to_find:
Check for privileged/root build containers:

```
grep -REn 'privileged:\s*true|--privileged|USER root|options:.*privileged' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Run build containers as non-root, without `--privileged`, drop capabilities, and avoid mounting the Docker socket. Re-review container options.

items.title: Unpatched runner images and toolchains
items.description: Outdated runner OS images, build tools, and agents accumulate known vulnerabilities that an attacker can exploit to escalate within the build environment.
items.how_to_find:
Check runner image/toolchain versions and patch status:

```
# review base image tags/age for self-hosted runners
```
items.how_to_fix: Keep runner images + toolchains patched (rebuild regularly from updated bases), scan them, and pin/refresh versions. Re-scan images.

items.title: Secrets printed to build logs
items.description: Echoed environment variables, verbose tool output, or set -x tracing can write secrets into build logs that are retained and often broadly readable.
items.how_to_find:
Search build logs / workflows for echoed secrets:

```
grep -REn 'echo .*secret|printenv|env\b|set -x' .github/workflows/ .gitlab-ci.yml
# review recent job logs for leaked values
```
items.how_to_fix: Never echo secrets, disable `set -x` around secret usage, rely on CI secret masking (and don't defeat it), and rotate any leaked secret. Re-review logs.

references.title: GitHub — Using secrets in GitHub Actions

items.title: Hardcoded secrets in source and pipeline config
items.description: API keys, tokens, and passwords committed into the repo or embedded in pipeline YAML are exposed to anyone with read access and persist in git history.
items.how_to_find:
Scan repo + pipeline files for secrets:

```
gitleaks detect --source . -v
grep -REn 'password|api[_-]?key|token|-----BEGIN' .github/ .gitlab-ci.yml
```
items.how_to_fix: Move secrets to the CI secret store / a secrets manager, reference them at runtime, add secret scanning to CI, and rotate exposed values. Re-scan.

references.title: GitHub — Using secrets in GitHub Actions

items.title: Secrets stored as plaintext CI variables
items.description: Storing credentials in unmasked/unprotected CI variables (rather than a secrets store) exposes them in the UI, in logs, and to lower-privileged users.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether CI variables holding secrets are marked masked/protected:

```
# GitLab: CI/CD variables -> Masked + Protected flags ; GH: use encrypted secrets not plain vars
```
items.how_to_fix: Store secrets as masked + protected (or in a secrets manager/OIDC), never as plain variables, and scope them to protected branches/environments. Re-verify variable settings.

items.title: Secrets baked into artifacts and images
items.description: Build steps that copy .env files, credentials, or tokens into container images or published artifacts ship secrets to anyone who pulls them.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect built images/artifacts for embedded secrets:

```
docker history --no-trunc <image>
dive <image> ; trivy image --scanners secret <image>
```
items.how_to_fix: Use multi-stage builds + build secrets (not `ARG`/layers), keep secrets out of artifacts, scan images for secrets in CI, and rotate exposed values. Re-scan artifacts.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Over-scoped tokens and PATs
items.description: Broad personal access tokens or registry/cloud tokens granted to pipelines amplify the impact of any leak — a single exposed token can reach far beyond its intended use.
items.how_to_find:
Review token/PAT scopes:

```
gh api /user | # for PATs, review granted scopes
# check deploy tokens, registry tokens, cloud tokens for excess scope
```
items.how_to_fix: Use fine-grained, least-privilege tokens scoped to specific repos/actions, prefer short-lived/OIDC over PATs, and expire them. Re-audit scopes.

references.title: GitHub — Using secrets in GitHub Actions

items.title: OIDC federation with loose subject conditions
items.description: Cloud OIDC trust policies that match too broadly (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Check cloud OIDC trust conditions for CI:

```
# AWS: review the IAM role trust policy's sub/aud conditions
aws iam get-role --role-name <ci-role> --query 'Role.AssumeRolePolicyDocument'
```

`sub` wildcards allowing any repo/branch = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Constrain OIDC trust to specific repo + branch/environment (`sub` with exact `repo:org/repo:ref:...`), verify `aud`, and least-privilege the assumed role. Re-review the trust policy.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening with OpenID Connect

items.title: Secret masking bypass
items.description: Masking that only catches exact strings can be bypassed by transforming a secret (base64, splitting, partial print), allowing exfiltration through logs.
items.how_to_find:
Check for ways masking is defeated (transformed/encoded/split secrets in logs):

```
grep -REn 'base64|echo.*|.*cut|tr ' .github/workflows/   # transformations that unmask
```
items.how_to_fix: Don't transform/print secrets (masking only matches exact values), avoid logging derived values, and rotate anything exposed. Re-review log handling.

references.title: GitHub — Using secrets in GitHub Actions

items.title: Secrets available to all repos and workflows
items.description: Org-level or globally available secrets exposed to every repository and workflow widen the blast radius — a compromise in any one repo can read them.
items.how_to_find:
Check secret scope (org-wide vs repo/environment):

```
gh api /orgs/<org>/actions/secrets | jq '.secrets[].visibility'
```

Org secrets visible to all repos = broad blast radius.
items.how_to_fix: Scope secrets to specific repos/environments (least exposure), use environment protection, and avoid org-wide secrets for sensitive credentials. Re-audit secret visibility.

references.title: GitHub — Using secrets in GitHub Actions

items.title: Long-lived, unrotated credentials
items.description: Static secrets that never expire or rotate stay valid indefinitely after a leak, maximizing the window an attacker has to use them.
items.how_to_find:
Find old static credentials:

```
# review creation/last-rotated dates of tokens, keys, deploy creds
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate credentials regularly, prefer short-lived/OIDC, set expiry on tokens/keys, and track rotation. Re-audit credential age.

items.title: Preference for short-lived credentials
items.description: Using OIDC/federated, short-lived credentials instead of stored long-lived secrets removes the standing secret an attacker could steal from the pipeline.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether pipelines use static long-lived creds vs short-lived OIDC/STS:

```
grep -REn 'AWS_ACCESS_KEY|aws-access-key|id-token:|permissions:.*id-token' .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Adopt OIDC/workload-identity federation for short-lived, auto-expiring credentials instead of stored static keys. Re-verify pipelines use OIDC.

references.title: GitHub — Security hardening with OpenID Connect

items.title: No revocation path for leaked credentials
items.description: Without secret scanning and a defined revoke-and-rotate response, a leaked credential discovered in code or logs remains live and exploitable.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether there's a documented, fast way to revoke a leaked secret:

```
# is there an inventory mapping secret -> owner -> revoke procedure?
```
items.how_to_fix: Maintain a secret inventory + tested revocation/rotation runbook, prefer revocable short-lived creds, and automate rotation on leak detection. Re-verify the runbook.

references.title: GitHub — Using secrets in GitHub Actions

items.title: Insufficient secret-scanning coverage
items.description: Secret scanning that does not cover all repos, history, and artifact build outputs leaves blind spots where leaked credentials go unnoticed.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether secret scanning runs on all repos + push protection is on:

```
gh api repos/<org>/<repo> | jq '.security_and_analysis'
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable secret scanning + push protection org-wide (and pre-commit hooks), scan history, and alert/rotate on hits. Re-verify coverage.

items.title: Dependency confusion / substitution
items.description: When internal package names are resolvable from public registries, an attacker can publish a higher-version public package that the build pulls instead of the internal one.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether internal package names could be resolved from public registries:

```
grep -REn 'registry|@<org>/|index-url|scope' package.json .npmrc pip.conf
```
items.how_to_fix: Reserve internal names on public registries, use scoped packages + a private registry/proxy with source pinning, and verify resolution order. Re-review registry config.

items.title: Typosquatted and malicious packages
items.description: Packages named to mimic popular libraries (or compromised maintainer accounts) introduce malicious code that executes during install or build.
items.how_to_find:
Review dependencies for typosquats / suspicious new packages:

```
npm ls --all ; # verify names/owners against expected
```
items.how_to_fix: Verify package names/owners, use an allowlisted proxy registry, review new deps + install scripts, and scan for malicious behavior. Re-audit deps.

items.title: Unpinned dependencies and missing lockfiles
items.description: Floating version ranges without a committed lockfile let a freshly published (possibly malicious) version be resolved on the next build without review.
items.how_to_find:
Check for version ranges / missing lockfiles:

```
grep -REn '\^|~|\*|latest' package.json requirements*.txt
ls package-lock.json poetry.lock go.sum 2>/dev/null
```
items.how_to_fix: Pin versions + commit lockfiles, install with `--frozen`/`npm ci` (integrity checks), and update via automation. Re-verify pinning.

items.title: Unmonitored transitive dependency risk
items.description: Most code in a build comes from transitive dependencies; without an SBOM and continuous scanning, a vulnerable or malicious transitive package goes unnoticed.
items.how_to_find:
Check transitive deps for vulns/malware:

```
osv-scanner -r . ; npm audit --all
```
items.how_to_fix: Include transitive deps in SCA, generate an SBOM, monitor continuously, and remediate high/critical. Re-scan.

sections.title: Dependencies & Supply Chain — Third-Party Services & Actions (CICD-SEC-8)

items.title: Unpinned third-party actions and plugins
items.description: Referencing an action by a mutable tag/branch (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Find actions pinned to mutable tags/branches:

```
grep -REn 'uses:\s*[^@]+@(v?\d|main|master)' .github/workflows/
```

`uses: someaction@v3` (tag, not SHA) can be moved under you.
items.how_to_fix: Pin third-party actions to a full commit SHA (not tags/branches), review before pinning, and update via automation with review. Re-audit `uses:` pins.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-8 — Ungoverned Usage of 3rd Party Services

items.title: Untrusted action publishers and marketplace risk
items.description: Adopting actions from unknown or unverified publishers grants third parties code execution and secret access inside your pipeline.
items.how_to_find:
Review third-party actions' publishers/source:

```
grep -REn 'uses:' .github/workflows/ | grep -v '<org>/'
```
items.how_to_fix: Prefer verified/first-party actions, vendor or fork critical ones, restrict allowed actions org-wide (allowlist), and review source. Re-audit action sources.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-8 — Ungoverned Usage of 3rd Party Services

items.title: Over-privileged third-party integrations
items.description: Installed apps and SaaS integrations granted broad org/repo and token scopes become a supply-chain entry point if the third party is breached.
items.how_to_find:
Review OAuth apps / GitHub Apps / integrations and their scopes:

```
gh api /orgs/<org>/installations | jq '.installations[] | {app_slug, permissions}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Least-privilege integration permissions, review + remove unused integrations, and require admin approval to install. Re-audit integrations.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-8 — Ungoverned Usage of 3rd Party Services

items.title: Transitive (nested) action dependencies
items.description: Actions that themselves call other actions extend the trust chain; an unpinned nested action can be compromised even if the top-level one is pinned.
items.how_to_find: Check whether used actions call other (unpinned) actions internally.
items.how_to_fix: Prefer actions with pinned internal deps, vendor critical actions, and pin your direct actions to SHAs (understanding they may pull nested deps). Re-review nested deps.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-8 — Ungoverned Usage of 3rd Party Services

sections.title: Dependencies & Supply Chain — Artifact Integrity & Provenance (CICD-SEC-9)

items.title: Unsigned build artifacts
items.description: Artifacts and container images published without signatures cannot be verified by consumers, so a tampered or substituted artifact is indistinguishable from a legitimate one.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether artifacts/images are signed:

```
cosign verify <image>   # fails if unsigned
```
items.how_to_fix: Sign artifacts/images (Siglstore/cosign) in the pipeline, and verify signatures before deploy (admission policy). Re-verify signing.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-9 — Improper Artifact Integrity Validation

items.title: Missing build provenance / attestation
items.description: Without provenance attestations (who built it, from which source and pipeline), consumers cannot verify an artifact’s origin, undermining SLSA assurances.
items.how_to_find:
Check for SLSA provenance/attestations on artifacts:

```
cosign verify-attestation --type slsaprovenance <image>
```
items.how_to_fix: Generate SLSA provenance/attestations in the build, and require verification before deploy. Re-verify attestations exist.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-9 — Improper Artifact Integrity Validation

items.title: Consuming artifacts by mutable tag, not digest
items.description: Pulling base images or artifacts by mutable tag (e.g.
items.how_to_find:
Find deployments referencing mutable tags (`:latest`) instead of digests:

```
grep -REn 'image:.*:latest|:v?\d+\b' k8s/ deploy/ .github/workflows/
```
items.how_to_fix: Reference artifacts/images by immutable digest (`@sha256:...`), and verify the digest matches a signed/attested build. Re-review deploy manifests.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-9 — Improper Artifact Integrity Validation

items.title: Weak artifact registry access and immutability
items.description: Registries that allow tag overwrites or broad push access let an attacker replace a published artifact with a malicious one under the same name.
items.how_to_find:
Check registry permissions + tag immutability:

```
# review who can push/overwrite tags; is tag immutability enabled?
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce tag immutability, least-privilege push access (separate from build), require signing, and audit registry access. Re-verify registry config.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-9 — Improper Artifact Integrity Validation

items.title: Publicly exposed CI dashboards and APIs
items.description: CI servers, build dashboards, and orchestration APIs reachable from the internet expose build status, logs, and often unauthenticated control, inviting takeover.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether the CI server/dashboard/API is internet-exposed:

```
nmap -p 8080,8443,50000 <ci-host>   # e.g. Jenkins
curl -s https://<ci-host>/api/json
```
items.how_to_fix: Put CI behind VPN/SSO + network restrictions, disable anonymous access, and don't expose management APIs publicly. Re-test exposure.

references.title: Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: Default or weak credentials on CI systems
items.description: CI orchestrators and plugins left with default admin credentials or weak auth give an attacker direct control of the build and deploy pipeline.
items.how_to_find:
Test CI systems for default/weak creds:

```
# try default admin creds on Jenkins/GitLab/registry admin panels
```

Default creds on CI = critical (pipeline takeover).
items.how_to_fix: Change all default credentials, enforce strong passwords + MFA/SSO, and disable default accounts. Re-test.

items.title: Outdated CI/CD platform and plugins
items.description: Unpatched CI servers and plugins (a recurring source of CVEs and RCE) let an attacker compromise the orchestrator and everything it deploys.
items.how_to_find:
Check CI platform + plugin versions for known CVEs:

```
# Jenkins: Manage Jenkins -> version + plugin updates ; GitLab version vs advisories
```
items.how_to_fix: Patch the CI platform + plugins promptly, remove unused plugins, and subscribe to advisories. Re-check versions.

items.title: Ungoverned and inconsistent platform settings
items.description: Without enforced org-wide policy, individual repos/projects drift to insecure settings (open runners, disabled protections), creating inconsistent and weak spots.
items.how_to_find:
Check for drift/inconsistent security settings across orgs/projects:

```
# compare branch protection / token defaults / runner settings across repos
```
items.how_to_fix: Manage platform settings as code (org rulesets/policy-as-code), enforce a consistent baseline, and detect drift. Re-audit for consistency.

items.title: Insufficient audit logging coverage
items.description: Missing audit logs for permission changes, secret access, pipeline edits, and deployments mean malicious actions in the pipeline go undetected.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether key CI/CD events are audited (config changes, secret access, deploys):

```
gh api /orgs/<org>/audit-log 2>/dev/null | head
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable audit logging for pipeline/config/secret/deploy events, cover all platforms, and review coverage. Re-verify.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-10 — Insufficient Logging and Visibility

items.title: No centralized log aggregation or retention
items.description: Logs kept only locally on CI systems can be wiped by an attacker and are unavailable for correlation; short retention destroys evidence before investigation.
items.how_to_find: Check whether CI logs/audit events ship to a central store with retention.
items.how_to_fix: Forward CI/CD + audit logs to a central SIEM with adequate retention + access control, and verify ingestion. Re-verify aggregation.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-10 — Insufficient Logging and Visibility

items.title: No detection or alerting on pipeline anomalies
items.description: Even with logs, the absence of alerting on suspicious events (new admin, secret exfil patterns, off-hours deploys) means attacks are only found after impact.
items.how_to_find: Check for alerting on suspicious pipeline activity (new secrets access, config changes, off-hours deploys).
items.how_to_fix: Add detections/alerts for anomalous pipeline behavior (unexpected secret access, workflow edits, new runners, deploy anomalies), and route to responders. Re-verify alerting.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-10 — Insufficient Logging and Visibility
Palo Alto — What is CI/CD security?

items.title: No runtime visibility into build execution
items.description: Without visibility into what build jobs actually do (network egress, process execution), poisoned pipeline execution and data exfiltration leave no observable trace.
items.how_to_find: Check whether build-time process/network activity is monitored (e.g. detecting exfil or unexpected downloads).
items.how_to_fix: Add runtime monitoring of build jobs (process/network egress, e.g. Falco/step-security-harden-runner), alert on anomalies, and restrict egress. Re-verify runtime visibility.

references.title: OWASP CICD-SEC-10 — Insufficient Logging and Visibility
